description: Fiserv is a large financial technology and payments infrastructure company serving merchants, financial institutions, fintechs, software platforms, and public-sector billers through acquiring, processing, banking, commerce, and point-of-sale products.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: Fiserv sits in the less glamorous but highly consequential middle of commerce and finance: the account-processing, card-issuing, merchant-acquiring, payments, e-commerce, and digital-banking infrastructure that lets money move without every checkout becoming a committee meeting.

Its customer base spans financial institutions, small businesses, large enterprises, fintechs, software providers, and public-sector organizations. That breadth makes Fiserv both a product company and a network operator, with value tied to transaction scale, reliability, integrations, and client trust.

overview.title: Core Product Surface
paragraphs: Clover is Fiserv's small-business operating platform, combining point-of-sale hardware, payment acceptance, online ordering, reporting, customer engagement, employee tools, and an app marketplace. It is the consumer-visible face of a much larger processing business.

Carat is the enterprise commerce platform, built for large merchants that need omnichannel acceptance, payment orchestration, optimization, fraud controls, data visibility, local payment methods, loyalty, and payout capabilities. The pitch is simple: abstract away payment complexity, then make the abstraction profitable.

overview.title: Infrastructure Position
paragraphs: Fiserv also provides core banking, digital banking, card issuer processing, network services, bill pay, merchant processing, and related financial-institution technology. Its bank-facing footprint gives it a position across both sides of the transaction: where consumers keep accounts and where merchants accept payments.

The company's scale is useful for the registry because it exposes several dependency points: proprietary merchant terminals, payment routing, banking-core platforms, closed network services, app ecosystems, data analytics, fraud tooling, and embedded finance rails.

overview.title: Research Watchlist
paragraphs: The current repo context has no technology waves mapped for Fiserv. Future review should consider whether embedded finance, merchant operating systems, payment orchestration, digital banking cores, real-time payments, stablecoin settlement, and AI-enabled financial operations are relevant enough to tag.

A Free The World profile should distinguish between Fiserv's visible products and its deeper infrastructure role. Clover and Carat are the clean product anchors, but the competitive and openness questions likely live in switching costs, data portability, payment network access, developer ecosystem limits, and contractual lock-in.
